upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a very shallow, moist boundary layer near the surface (spread only 1.6°C at 1017mb) with a strong low-level temperature inversion: surface temp 11°C jumps to 15.4°C at 850mb (1524m), indicating a well-defined marine inversion capping convection at roughly 1500m MSL. Above the inversion, lapse rates are modest and thermals will be weak-to-moderate at best. Upper winds are significant: 20-21 kts from 300° at 850mb, increasing to 34 kts at 700mb (3146m), indicating strong wind shear above the inversion layer that will cap usable thermal height well below 850mb. The sfc→850mb lapse rate of -2.76°C/km is extremely stable, suggesting thermals will struggle to develop and top out low.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area SF region. KHAF reporting 3-4SM visibility in mist (BR) with calm winds — marine layer/fog at the coast this morning, slow clearing expected. KSFO calm winds at 10SM, KRHV calm and clear, KAPC calm and clear. Buoy SF Bar 46026 reporting 13.6 kts from 330° and Half Moon Bay 46012 reporting 11.7 kts from 330° — NW swell pattern, SSTs cold at 11.6-12.6°C supporting a persistent marine layer.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland hills, NW-facing launch, thermal and ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin 1750ft is primarily a thermal site that also benefits from the afternoon valley sea breeze (NW-W flow). Ideal conditions are a developing NW-W breeze of 8-15 kts at launch with thermals cycling up from the sun-heated valley below.
sites.todaySetup: The main limiting factor today is a very shallow, stable boundary layer driven by the strong low-level inversion confirmed in the KOAK sounding — thermals are capped well below the 1750ft launch height, with BL heights peaking at only 465-495m AGL per model. Winds at the 1750ft level are light NNE-NNW at 5-9 kts this morning, slowly backing and weakening through the afternoon. KRHV METAR shows calm winds and clear skies at the valley floor, which is favorable for thermal initiation, but the stable air mass will limit thermal height and strength significantly. Tomorrow.io shows gusts reaching 13-14 kts at the 1750ft level between 3-5 PM, which is somewhat punchy for the otherwise light mean wind, suggesting variable thermal activity with occasional strong pulses. Cloud cover builds to 100% after 4 PM (models agree), effectively killing any remaining thermal activity and making late afternoon flights inadvisable.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light NNE winds 7-9 kts at launch, BL only 285-475m. Too early for thermals to develop meaningfully; air is stable and the inversion is firmly in place. Not a viable flying window.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds backing to NNW at 5-7 kts, BL height peaks around 465-495m — marginal thermal development possible but capped low. CAPE is zero, thermals will be weak and short. Flyable for experienced pilots willing to work low saves, but expect low ceilings.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds shift NW-WNW 6-8 kts, but cloud cover builds rapidly to 100% by 4 PM, shutting down solar heating entirely. Tomorrow.io gusts to 13-14 kts in this window add punch. Window closes by 4 PM at latest due to overcast.

verdict.hazards: Low thermal ceiling (~465m AGL), strong inversion capping flights, gusty pulses to 14 kts in afternoon, 100% cloud cover by 4 PM

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — mid-hill launch, NW-facing, beginner/intermediate site
sites.howItWorks: The 600ft launch at Ed Levin is a lower alternative used in lighter conditions or as a training hill. It benefits from the same valley thermal and sea breeze cycle but is more sheltered and suitable for lower-wind days. Ideal wind is 8-12 kts NW-W.
sites.todaySetup: The 600ft launch faces a challenging day due to extremely light and variable surface winds through midday — models show only 2-4 kts with variable direction (NNW to W) until early afternoon, well below what's needed for reliable launches. BL height at this lower elevation peaks around 480-495m AGL, which is marginally above the launch but the air is very stable with zero CAPE. Tomorrow.io shows gusts spiking to 9-11 kts between 4-7 PM at the 600ft level — light mean winds but punchy gusts indicate thermal activity is turbulent and unreliable. The model disagreement between HRRR/GFS (both light NW) and Tomorrow.io (light mean with punchy gusts) suggests variable, mixing-driven conditions in the afternoon rather than a clean sea breeze. Overall, this is a marginal day at best for the 600ft launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds very light 2-4 kts, variable direction. BL below 370m. Too calm and stable for reliable soaring; thermal cycles weak and inconsistent.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds gradually build to 4-5 kts NW-WNW as afternoon heating begins. BL rises to ~480m. Short thermal cycles possible but ceilings are low; only a small soaring window for patient P3 pilots.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 5-7 kts NW with gusts to 9-11 kts per Tomorrow.io — light mean but punchy gusts make conditions variable and not suitable for P2 pilots. Cloud cover moves in by 4-5 PM.

verdict.hazards: Very light winds, punchy gusts to 11 kts in afternoon, low thermal ceiling, overcast by 4 PM

sites.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — lower training hill, W-facing, training site
sites.howItWorks: The 300ft launch is a training hill used for ground handling and sledders in light conditions. It relies on light W-WNW flow and mild thermal activity for soaring. Being the lowest launch, it is most sheltered but also most affected by valley turbulence.
sites.todaySetup: Today's 300ft launch conditions are actually the most coherent of the three Ed Levin launches from a wind direction standpoint — models show a progressive W-WNW flow developing from 4-8 kts between 11 AM and 4 PM, which is on-heading for this launch. However, the BL tops out at 410-415m AGL, barely above the launch site, meaning thermal ceilings are extremely low. Tomorrow.io shows very light gusts (5 kts) which is consistent with a calm, stable day. The primary issue is that light winds and a capped, stable atmosphere mean soaring will be limited to short sledder runs or very brief thermal cycles close to the hill. This is actually the most appropriate site for P2 pilots today given the gentle conditions, but expectations should be set for sledder flights rather than extended soaring.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light W-NW flow 3-4 kts. Boundary layer just 25-305m AGL. Stable air, suitable for ground handling practice or short sledder flights only.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: W-WNW flow builds to 5-7 kts as valley heating picks up. BL height rises to 360-380m. Short soaring possible on thermal cycles; best window of the day for this launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind builds to 7-8 kts WNW by 3-4 PM, then cloud cover arrives. Tomorrow.io gusts reach only 5 kts here, indicating a relatively benign afternoon, but the overcast by 4 PM ends any thermal activity quickly.

verdict.hazards: Very low thermal ceiling, stable inversion, overcast by 4 PM; sledder day more likely than extended soaring

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal ridge, W-facing, ridge/sea breeze site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge soaring site requiring consistent W-WNW sea breeze at 10-20 kts for sustained lift. It faces directly into the prevailing Pacific sea breeze. Marine layer fog and light/variable winds are the primary killers. Ideal: W at 12-18 kts, clear of marine layer.
sites.todaySetup: Today at Mussel Rock is marginal-to-no-fly, driven by two compounding problems: the marine layer and insufficient wind strength. KHAF METAR is reporting 3-4SM visibility in mist with dead-calm winds as of mid-morning — the marine layer is sitting right at coast level. Buoy SSTs are cold (11.6-12.6°C) with NW swell at 7s period and NW winds of 11-14 kts offshore, indicating a well-established marine layer that will be slow to break. Models show winds at the site only 4-6 kts W through the morning and early afternoon — too light for reliable ridge soaring at this cliff-edge site. There's a small window around 1-2 PM where HRRR shows 6.3 kts W before clouds arrive at 2-3 PM. Tomorrow.io is more optimistic with gusts to 5-6 kts but mean winds below 5 kts — not enough for sustained soaring. The 1% precip chance from 2-7 PM adds to the concern. Overall, with marginal wind, likely lingering marine mist, and cloud cover building mid-afternoon, today is predominantly a NO FLY day at Mussel Rock.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: KHAF reporting mist and calm winds — marine layer still present at the coast. Site winds 4 kts W per model; too light and too foggy for safe soaring. No fly.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best chance of the day: winds may build to 5-6 kts W by 12-1 PM as marine layer partially lifts. Still below ideal threshold for reliable ridge soaring. Experienced local P3+ pilots may find brief ridge lift but it's very marginal.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Cloud cover jumps to 99-100% by 3 PM and light precip (1%) is possible. Winds hold 6-7 kts W but overcast conditions and possible drizzle make this unflyable.

verdict.hazards: Marine layer/mist at coast (KHAF 3-4SM BR), wind below ridge soaring threshold all day, cloud cover 99-100% by 3 PM, light precip possible

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland peak, NW-facing, thermal and ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak soars on a combination of thermal lift from the heated Fremont/Tri-Valley plain and NW-W sea breeze ridge lift. The peak at ~2517ft needs NW-W flow at 8-15 kts for good ridge conditions; thermals can develop strong on warm sunny days.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak faces the same fundamental problem as Ed Levin — the strong low-level inversion confirmed in the KOAK sounding caps the thermal ceiling at around 415-475m AGL, well below what's needed for extended soaring from this higher launch. Model winds at the launch are notably light-to-moderate NNW at 5-9 kts through midday, consistent across both HRRR and GFS with no meaningful disagreement. Tomorrow.io shows even lighter mean winds (1-5 kts) at Mission Peak through the afternoon with gusts only reaching 10 kts by 7 PM, suggesting the valley breeze cycle is weak and unreliable today. KRHV confirms calm conditions on the valley floor as of this morning, so thermal triggers are present but the stable air mass will limit cycle quality. The rapid cloud build to 100% after 4 PM (both models agree) kills the afternoon window. There's a narrow marginal window between 11 AM and 3 PM for experienced pilots willing to work low in stable air.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNE-N winds 7-9 kts at the peak, 13 kts at 80m level — wind shear and BL only 285-475m. Too early, thermals not established, directional uncertainty. Not a viable window.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds lighten to NNW 5-7 kts, BL peaks at 465-475m. A narrow window for experienced pilots — expect weak thermals, low ceilings, and short flights. Tomorrow.io's light surface readings suggest the sea breeze has not filled in strongly.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds back to WNW 5-6 kts but 100% cloud cover arrives by 4 PM, shutting down all solar thermal activity. Tomorrow.io gusts reach 8-10 kts by 5-7 PM but in overcast conditions — not useful for soaring.

verdict.hazards: Strong low-level inversion capping thermals below 475m AGL, 100% overcast by 4 PM, gusty pulses to 10 kts in weak thermal air, low soaring ceiling

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Tuesday looks marginally better in the morning with NNE flow 6-7 kts, but models show winds backing to NNW-WNW 7-9 kts by afternoon with BL rising to 380-410m. Still stable with zero CAPE. Cloud cover builds to 100% by 4 PM again. A similar marginal day — small soaring window 11 AM to 3 PM for P3+.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Similar pattern: calm morning, light NNW 2-4 kts midday, building to WNW 7-10 kts by afternoon with gusts to 11 kts. BL tops at 410m. The afternoon sea breeze push looks slightly stronger than today, but still marginal. P3+ only, 12-3 PM window.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: W-WNW flow 5-9 kts develops by midday, slightly stronger than today. BL to 415m. Best of the three Ed Levin launches tomorrow — light, clean W flow makes it appropriate for P2 pilots in the 11 AM – 3 PM window before clouds move in.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Tomorrow morning starts with near-calm offshore winds (models show 0.7-1.8 kts at 8-10 AM) — likely marine layer present again. Sea breeze fills in by noon at 5.8-8 kts W, building to 9.4 kts by 4 PM. A real afternoon sea breeze window may open 12-3 PM if marine layer clears. HRRR and GFS agree on this pattern. P3+ locals, watch the morning marine layer clearing before committing.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Very similar to today: NNE 6-7 kts morning, backing to WNW 7-8 kts afternoon, BL 370-415m, 100% clouds by 4 PM. Marginal thermal window 11 AM – 2 PM. No meaningful improvement over Monday for P3+ pilots.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Wednesday (Mar 25) shows 100% cloud cover all day with light W-WNW winds 1-9 kts. BL height is wider (490-815m) suggesting less stable air, but full overcast kills solar thermal development entirely. Not a flying day.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Same 100% overcast all day Wednesday with light to moderate WNW winds. BL height rises to 815m in the afternoon but is irrelevant without solar heating. No fly.

day2.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day2.analysis: Full overcast Wednesday, same conditions as upper launches. Light WNW winds, no thermal development possible. No fly.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Wednesday shows increasing W-WNW winds reaching 11.8-13.8 kts by afternoon — approaching ridge soaring threshold. However, 100% cloud cover all day means gloomy, possibly drizzly conditions. Could be an interesting day for bold local ridge pilots if precip holds off, but visibility concerns are real.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Wednesday full overcast, light W-NW flow 2-5 kts at the surface with stronger winds aloft (11-13 kts at 80m). Stable, overcast day with no thermal potential. No fly.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Thursday (Mar 26) remains 100% overcast with light NNW 4-7 kts and BL heights of 375-800m. No solar heating, no thermals. Another no-fly day inland.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Thursday same overcast pattern, light NNW flow, no improvement in flyability. No fly.

day3.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day3.analysis: Thursday overcast with light NNW 4-7 kts. Possibly usable for ground handling practice but no soaring window.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Thursday shows an interesting pattern: W-NW flow building to 9.2 kts by 2 PM with BL heights reaching 615-815m under 100% overcast. Ridge lift may be marginally possible in the afternoon if drizzle/precip stays light, but overall conditions remain compromised by full cloud cover. Watch for any clearing.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Thursday shows NE-ENE flow 4-7 kts — atypical direction, possibly indicating an offshore flow pattern developing. BL to 735m but 100% overcast. Wrong wind direction for this site. No fly.

watchlist: Marine layer at KHAF reporting 3-4SM mist and calm winds through mid-morning — coastal sites (Mussel Rock) need to wait for confirmed clearing before any flight attempt
Cold SSTs (11.6-12.6°C) at SF Bar and Half Moon Bay buoys indicate a persistent, deep marine layer that will be slow to erode at Mussel Rock all week
Strong low-level inversion confirmed in KOAK sounding (surface 11°C vs 15.4°C at 850mb) is the dominant feature today — caps thermal height at all inland sites below 500m AGL
Tomorrow.io showing gusty pulses of 10-14 kts at Ed Levin 1750ft in the 3-5 PM window despite light mean winds — punchy thermals in stable air are a hazard especially for P2 pilots
100% cloud cover arriving at all sites by 4 PM today per both HRRR and GFS models — hard cutoff for any afternoon flying window
Multi-day overcast pattern developing from Wednesday through at least Thursday — next good flying window may not arrive until late week or weekend
tldr: Today is a marginal day across the board — Ed Levin 300ft offers the cleanest conditions for P2 pilots with a light W flow window between 11 AM and 3 PM, while the 1750ft and Mission Peak launches have a narrow P3+ window in the same timeframe before clouds shut everything down by 4 PM. Mussel Rock is a no-fly with marine layer mist on the coast and winds below soaring threshold. Fly early if you're going, because the atmosphere is capped low and the overcast arrives fast.
